summary: Jeff Atwood announces he's disabling trackbacks on his blog due to overwhelming spam, explaining that what was once a promising technology for linking conversations across blogs has become utterly broken. The trackback specification, published by Six Apart in 2002, lacked any authentication mechanism, making it trivially easy for spammers to abuse. Despite trying blacklists and other countermeasures for years, Atwood concedes defeat as spammers can register domains and mass-post faster than anyone can block them. He's replacing trackbacks with Technorati's link indexing service as a workaround, while emphasizing that social software designers must account for malicious users from the start.
key_points: Trackbacks were designed to create coherent conversations across different blogs by automatically linking related posts
The original Six Apart trackback specification had no authentication mechanism, making abuse trivially easy
CAPTCHA solved comment spam but is useless for trackbacks since they're machine-entered by design
Band-aid solutions like moderation queues and distributed blacklists aren't sustainable against determined spammers
Social software must be designed from day one with the assumption that some users will be malicious
money_quotes: I often am full of crap.
Trackbacks are a way of relating conversations across websites.
Unfortunately, trackbacks are so horribly and fundamentally broken that they're effectively useless.
CAPTCHA has completely solved my comment spam problem. But distinguishing between humans and machines is useless on trackbacks, which are all machine entered by definition.
These are only slightly larger band-aids over a massive, sucking chest wound.
Trackbacks, as we currently know them, are dead, kaput, expired.
That's what happens when you design social software without considering the impact of malicious users from the very beginning.
Design from day one with the assumption that a few of your users will be evil.
key_insight: Social software that doesn't account for malicious users in its initial design will inevitably be exploited and rendered useless, no matter how good the original concept.
